items.evidence: The interview opens 'We're in Summit, with Rev. Wheeler Parker Jr.' and the narrator later states 'Move to Summit (Argo) right outside Chicago, in 1947.' Argo is a 1907 company-town subdivision built around the Corn Products plant (maker of Argo Corn Starch) and was annexed into the Village of Summit in 1911; locals still call the area 'Summit-Argo.' The Chicago Sun-Times confirms 'The Parker family moved to the south suburban Argo community in January 1947' and that Wheeler Parker today pastors the Argo Temple Church of God in Christ in Summit. The 'Cuddahy' option in the original candidate is a phonetic guess (Cudahy is a Milwaukee suburb, not a Chicago one) and is not supported. The correct rendering is 'Summit (Argo), Illinois.'

Parker explains how Rich Samuels of NBC came to do a 1985 Emmett Till documentary: 'As he met Miss Teal [Mamie Till], as a young basketball player, had been killed, Benjamin "Ben" Wilson Jr. He was a Chicago and she was there for support.' Benjamin 'Ben' Wilson Jr. (1967-1984) was the nationally top-ranked high-school basketball player at Simeon Career Academy in Chicago, shot on Nov. 20, 1984 and died Nov. 21, 1984; his mother and Mamie Till-Mobley's public-grief connection is well documented. The NBC-affiliate WMAQ-TV documentary 'The Murder and the Movement: The Emmett Till Story' (written by Rich Samuels) aired in July 1985, consistent with Parker's account. 'Beauford' is a Whisper phonetic error for 'Ben/Benjamin.'
